tweet: Over the last year, @SenAngusKing and I have spent a lot of time referring to Solarium as a 9/11 Commission before 9/11. But the past few months have challenged this framing. The cyber disruption is already here, and it is happening on a near daily basis.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: We need to explore how we can provide liability protections for companies to respond in real time against cyberattacks while doubling down on Solarium recommendations to expand federal network defense and public-private collaboration.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Yet these steps will only help if we action them. We need nominations for the National Cyber Director and CISA Director yesterday. We need a declaratory policy when it comes cyberattacks, and we need to signal that we will enforce it ruthlessly.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Thanks to the CSC's tireless efforts and bipartisan collaboration on both sides of the Hill, the FY 21 NDAA was the most significant cyber legislation in US history. We created the National Cyber Director, strengthened CISA, and took steps to ensure continuity of our economy.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: President Biden is blowing through norms with the sheer volume and scope of these executive orders. That's wrong. But it shouldn't be surprising. The next president, regardless of party, will probably do the same thing.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: That hurts the economy, it sows confusion among citizens, and it often helps the powerful and the connected at the expense of everybody else. That, in turn, raises the stakes for each election - because seemingly everything turns on the outcome.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: In the short term, this behavior wins the praise of partisans. But in the longer term it creates instability and chaos in our constitutional system. Every single election, the disposition of the entire federal government changes on a dime, across a wide range of issues.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: So what happens when Congress decides not to play its part? First, the other branches step in to fill the space. Courts legislate from the bench. The president and bureaucracy tackle problems and policy however they like.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Our constitution is designed to have Congress at the center. We are supposed to be making laws and directing the government. The president and the federal courts are empowered to check Congress, but they are not supposed to replace our legislative authority.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: This is THE biggest problem with Congress today. You may have heard that America has three coequal branches. This is wrong. Congress is the dominant branch of government. We are the first branch, and we have the most power over the other two.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Put simply: Congress. Every president issues guidance to the executive branch. That's normal. But when Congress passes bad, vague, or imprecise laws, it opens up space that executive orders fill. In other words, Congress enables presidential discretion.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Cutting our forces in Germany not only reduces our forward presence and our ability to deter aggression in Europe, but it will end up costing us more. Troops leaving Germany will have to go somewhere, and wherever they go, they won’t have the benefit of German financial support.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: To deny, we must be forward. Our forces in Germany are a critical lynchpin of this forward presence. Not only does Germany have unparalleled supporting infrastructure, but it provides millions of dollars to help fund the US presence.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: The National Defense Strategy rightly prioritizes these forward forces today. Rather than punish adversary aggression after the fact, the NDS emphasizes a posture of deterrence by denial, which seeks to deny adversaries their attempted conquests in the first place.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: The war was a terrible lesson that American needed to defend forward. No longer could we sit back across the ocean and let the storm clouds gather. In the ensuing decades, our forward presence won the Cold War by successfully deterring a Soviet invasion of Western Europe.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: The extraordinary sacrifices made on that day of days did not come out of thin air. In the 1930s, deterrence failed, our adversaries saw a free world unwilling to impose costs on aggression, and democracies were late to mobilize against the Nazi war machine.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: THREAD ➡️ 76 years ago today, tens of thousands of Americans, along with our allies, displayed unparalleled courage on the beaches and causeways of Normandy, beginning the long process of liberating Europe from Nazi rule.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: As a Marine, you learn to ride to the sound of the guns. When setting goals as a civilian, exercise that same mentality. Once you’re discharged, go where the action is. Push yourself outside your comfort zone & use the skills you’ve learned to achieve what you want. #VeteransChat

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Even with a strong support network, I skipped simple tasks and didn’t take advantage of all the resources available to me at the time.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: I would’ve looked for more help. When I discharged, I was eager to start my life. But I didn’t realize just how many resources were available to me in the form of VA benefits and local VSOs. #VeteransChat

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Incidentally, this ethos of selfless service is what makes veterans such an asset in any workplace. We can work in teams to get difficult jobs done. #VeteransChat

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Officers eat last. We have a duty to help others, to look out for our subordinates first. Particularly those of us who have benefitted from great mentorship in our careers, I believe we have a duty to pay that back. #VeteransChat

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Be open, be honest, and let your mentor know when you need help. Your mentor has been in your shoes — he or she knows the ins and outs of transitioning back to civilian life and can show you the ropes through even the littlest of matters. #VeteransChat

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: You’ll quickly learn there’s a lot of help out there — whether it’s job assistance or free donut Fridays at a local business.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: That’s why I firmly believe that the best resource for veterans are their fellow service members. When you get back, connect with your veteran service officer, your local veteran service organization, or a non-profit that focuses on helping veterans.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Don't be embarrassed to ask for help. Drop the tough guy/gal act and seek assistance (i.e. leadership principle: know yourself and seek self-improvement). There are many resources available to us as veterans — we just have to be willing to ask and learn about them. #VeteransChat

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: As veterans, we have incredible skill sets and leadership traits from our time in service. But it’s on us to translate those skills into success in the civilian world. #VeteransChat

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: The belief that our military experience will fit nicely with every civilian job is a common blind spot, in large part because not every civilian company understands fully the nature of military experience or the specific skills we gain from our military occupational specialties.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Often we don't realize how overwhelming some of the basics of civilian life can be until after we are discharged. That alone can be a difficult adjustment. #VeteransChat

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Consider how many choices the military makes for you when you're on active duty. Basic things like monthly financial planning, getting a loan for a house, or starting to save for your kids' school may not even cross your radar if you're living on base and deploying all the time.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Recently discharged veterans, myself included, tend to think that the transition to civilian life will be easy. For many, it’s not. Veterans should accept advice during their transition and understand that civilian life comes with challenges we just don't face in the military.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Veterans in my area do a remarkable job of helping others find peer mentors. Groups like the NEW Battalion are “the missing link for the transitioning process” and show how veterans support each other as civilians just as they did in combat. #VeteransChat  https://t.co/TvQIcKrRoZ

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: As veterans, we have different experiences and speak a different language than our civilian counterparts — that can be a difficult adjustment. Having a mentor is vital to better understanding how to operate in the business world, and to bridging the gap we may otherwise have.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: 10. Find strength in our community. Northeast Wisconsin’s strength is the quality and heart of this community. We all need to do our part to help our neighbors, support local nonprofits and businesses, and get through this the way we do best—together.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: 9. Reopen state parks, with caveats. More than ever, this is a time where Wisconsinites need to be able to get fresh air and a dose of sanity. Whether it’s closing parking lots, maintaining limited hours, or keeping a skeleton ranger crew on hand, we need to get creative.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: 8. Come up with a bipartisan election plan, now. The August primary will be here before we know it, and soon after, November. We should start immediately planning to ensure they go off without a hitch — the legitimacy of our democracy is at stake.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: 7. Focus on our dairy farmers. Wisconsin’s dairy sector desperately needs help. Federal funds are a start, but until we break the bottleneck at the processor level, we will be stuck seeing rationing at grocery stores while our farmers are forced to dump excess milk.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: 6. School’s in for summer. Our students can’t afford to lose a half year or more of schooling. High schools should consider more online classes, while younger classes may want to make use of empty high schools to move to in person instruction in significantly smaller groups.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: 5. Continue to support small business. Congress desperately needs to add resources to the Paycheck Protection Program, which ran through $350 billion in two weeks. Every day we delay putting more money into this program is a day we risk seeing hardworking businesses go under.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: 4. Maintain strict social distancing for the most at risk populations, including the elderly and those with compromised immune systems. We’ll have to continue pitching in to help these individuals, as they may not be able to return to normal until a vaccine is widely available.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: 3. Develop responsible contact tracing and antibody analysis for individuals who have been infected. The private sector is doing great work in this regard, and we need to leverage these resources and put them to work on behalf of Wisconsinites.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: 2. Testing, testing, and more testing. The sooner our labs and hospitals have the ability to provide quick and accessible tests to a majority of Wisconsinites, the sooner we can end the lockdown.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: 1. We need a bipartisan task force charged with creating a plan to reopen WI. The task force would bring everyone to the table—state authorities, healthcare experts, businesses large and small, first responders, and educators—and get to work immediately. There’s no time to waste.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: This is no time for partisan theatrics. It's time to continue what is working, fix what isn't, support our neighbors, and get through this as we always have--as a community.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: But while we need to fix these pressing issues, we also can’t let this distract from defeating this disease. There aren’t enough dollars to print if we fail to contain this virus, and any additional action should surge more resources and support to the frontlines of the fight.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: I'm glad to see initial reports indicating Congress could add to the pool of PPP resources available for small businesses. Across NEW, small businesses are desperately awaiting the help they need to meet payroll, make rent, and survive another day. Relief cannot come soon enough.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Third, despite Congressional intent, SBA has severely restricted non-payroll loan forgiveness in order to maximize the number of businesses that can benefit from the program. To fix this, Congress should add more funding AND provide more flexibility in how the loans can be used.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Second, we need to support startups—some of which may help prevent future pandemics—by ensuring there’s no confusion around the SBA's affiliation rules. SBA should ensure lenders have the clarity they need to expeditiously make loans to innovative startups.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: At the same time, we still have to address outstanding issues. For one, some lenders are unsure whether PPP eligibility definitions exclude many NEW dairy farms from receiving support. SBA should immediately clarify that dairy farms do not have to meet SBA’s NAICS codes.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: While anything on the scale of the CARES Act will take time, Northeast Wisconsin's small businesses do not have weeks to wait. We must be laser-focused on processing PPP loans as quickly as possible in order to get cash into the hands of those who need it most--yesterday.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: THREAD ➡️ Before speculation about the next Coronavirus relief package gets out of hand, Congress must pause efforts to include special interest projects and ideological pipe dreams. Instead, we need to devote our attention to implementing and fixing what is already on the books.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: As a maritime nation, our physical security begins and ends with command of the seas. Maritime superiority is not a birthright but a hard-won status we can’t surrender. The task ahead for the Navy and advocates in Congress is not easy, but it is simple: don’t give up the ship.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: In other words, we need a course correction, at flank speed. Front page incidents are starting to pile up, taking with them close to a generation of seasoned leaders. Morale is down. Budgets are inadequate. Enemies are investing heavily in hard power, particularly seapower.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Even as investigations continue, the Navy must turn the page. That starts with Senate-confirmed leadership at the top, a commitment to transparency, and a willingness to communicate a simple vision of geopolitically-informed, integrated American seapower directly to the public.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: While I am grateful for his service, it was no longer tenable for Secretary Modly to lead the Navy. Many Americans rightly have questions about the state of their Navy, and it is imperative that we understand the events that led to Capt. Crozier sending his letter on March 30.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: THREAD ➡️ The past week has been extraordinarily difficult for our Navy. I know the frustration that many sailors might be feeling right now, & I hope they know a grateful nation has their back. America asks its Navy to make incredible sacrifices daily & they do not go unnoticed.

tweet: I hope we can learn from this. We have enough problems without inventing bogeymen—either from those who repeatedly peddled the collusion narrative or from those who baselessly attacked Mueller’s integrity. We need to work together to ensure this does not become business as usual.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Key individuals who should have known better lied to the public, and in doing so, did serious harm to our national security. They disgraced themselves and, unfortunately, damaged the reputations of their institutions and the many honorable men and women in the IC.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: As a former intelligence officer, I’m gravely concerned by the way the intelligence community (IC) became politicized over the past two years. Officials like Comey, Clapper, Brennan, and Schiff eroded public trust in our IC by wading too deeply into politics and innuendo.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: The simple fact is our media did itself no favors over the past two years. All too often, it delved into clickbait conspiracy rather than objective journalism. This can’t continue, because we really do need the media to hold politicians accountable.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Just imagine for a minute being so opposed to the President that you actively wished he was disloyal to the country and was an agent of a hostile foreign power. It’s crazy and demonstrates the extent to which shallow political combat has become our new national religion.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Which leads to another ugly truth. All Americans should be glad there was no collusion. But you wouldn’t know it by all the disappointed faces on cable news, some of whom are now questioning the integrity of Mueller and Barr.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: Part of getting to work means coming to terms with the ugly truths underlying this saga and ensuring something like this never happens again. That means we not only need full disclosure of the report, but also the process that gave rise to the investigation in the first place.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: @POTUS 7/7: We need to give the North Korean people that most dangerous of weapons—hope. The Kim regime cannot last forever. We must empower and give strength to those courageous enough to push for change.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: @POTUS 6/7: The Kim regime is built on a series of lies about the outside world, and no lie is strong enough to last forever. Until the Kim regime one day falls, America will be on the side of the North Korean people, and not their corrupt and abusive leaders.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: @POTUS 5/7: We seek not to impose our way of life on North Korea, but rather, to see all Koreans be able to one day express themselves in an open society of their own choosing, with leaders accountable to the population.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: @POTUS 4/7: The President should use his platform to make clear that the American people do not desire war with North Korea. What we desire is that all the Korean people live free, prosperous, and peaceful lives. The US is not alone: many in South Korea and Japan agree!

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: @POTUS 3/7: @POTUS could turn a source of frequent criticism into a catalyst for change in North Korea that would benefit the US & our allies. His message would assuredly reach into North Korea, just as all revolutionary ideas throughout history have not been confined by borders.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: @POTUS 2/7: I know, few people think the White House should spend more time on Twitter. And North Koreans don’t have access to Twitter in the first place.

name: Rep. Mike Gallagher
tweet: 1/7: While @POTUS is in South Korea tomorrow, he should take the opportunity to speak to the North Korean people via Twitter, much like President Reagan bypassed the Soviet regime to speak to the people of the Soviet Union.   So, you’re probably thinking... Twitter? Hear me out.
